title: Empanada Gallega
subtitle: Galician tuna and pepper pie
excerpt: A large flat pie of olive oil dough filled with tuna, slow-softened onions, red pepper, and smoked paprika. Eaten at room temperature, sliced into squares, and carried to picnics and markets.
sourceNotes: Empanada gallega is the most celebrated version of the Spanish filled pie, originating in Galicia. The olive oil dough (not butter or lard) is characteristic of the region. Documented in Claudia Roden, The Food of Spain (2011) and Penelope Casas, The Foods and Wines of Spain (1982).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked, wrapped tightly, for up to 2 months. Defrost overnight and eat at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before and eaten at room temperature. The flavours improve overnight.

content.text: The dough for empanada gallega is made with olive oil rather than butter, which gives it a sturdy, slightly chewy texture that holds up to slicing and carrying. It should not be flaky or crumbly. The filling is a sofrito of slowly cooked onion, red pepper, tomato purée, and smoked paprika, into which tinned tuna is folded — the filling must be dry, not wet, or the base will not cook through.

Make the dough and filling separately, then bring them together. The dough needs an hour's rest before rolling; the filling needs 25 minutes of slow cooking to lose enough liquid. Neither step can be rushed.

Mix the flour and salt in a large bowl. Add the olive oil and warm water and mix until a soft, smooth dough forms — it should not be sticky. Knead briefly for 2 minutes. Wrap and rest for 1 hour at room temperature.

Warm the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-low heat. Add the onion and a pinch of salt and cook, stirring occasionally, for 15 minutes until soft and sweet. Add the red pepper and cook for a further 10 minutes. Add the tomato purée and paprika and stir for 2 minutes. Remove from the heat, fold in the drained tuna, and season. The mixture must be cool and relatively dry before it goes into the pie.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Divide the dough into two pieces, one slightly larger. Roll the larger piece into a rectangle to fit your baking tray. Transfer to a lightly oiled tray. Spread the filling evenly, leaving a 2 cm border. Roll the second piece of dough slightly larger than the filling, lay it on top, and press the edges firmly together. Crimp or fold to seal. Brush with beaten egg and cut a small steam vent in the centre. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until deep golden.

items.fix: Cook the filling until very dry; if there is still liquid in the pan after adding the tuna, cook for 5 more minutes over low heat

items.cause: Not rested long enough; gluten is tight
items.fix: Rest for the full hour; roll gently, starting from the centre outward

content.text: Empanada gallega is festival food — carried to romerías (country pilgrimages), sold at markets, eaten cold at picnics. Variations include sardine, crab, lamb with onion, and pork with peppers. The tuna version is the most common and travels best. In Galicia it is sold by the square; nobody orders it by the slice.
